frameworks.name: Bayesian belief updating
frameworks.one_line: unified probabilistic account of how past decisions reshape beliefs about decision variables

frameworks.name: decisional history effects
frameworks.one_line: classical attractive serial dependence extended by a granularity-dependent pull

core_question: How does committing to a decision update an observer's belief about the underlying decision variable, and does choice granularity modulate this update?
key_assumptions: humans form latent decision variables during perceptual decisions
commitment to a choice restricts the range of admissible beliefs about that variable
manipulations: granularity of the choice set (coarser vs finer response categories)
psychophysical task structure across seven experiments
key_findings: the attractive pull is stronger when prior choices were made with finer granularity (granularity effect)
a single Bayesian model jointly captures granularity effect and known history effects
interpretations: decision commitment acts as an inference step that constrains the belief on the decision variable
humans exploit probabilistic inference on decision variables to adapt to the task environment
